results.title: Adversary can make honest parties unable to retrieve their assertion stakes if the required amount is decreased
results.reasoning: Yes. The report’s Issue #24 (Protocol Insolvency via loserStakeEscrow Accounting Desync) describes the same code path and underlying flaw in RollupUserLogic: on creating a non–first-child assertion, stake is transferred to loserStakeEscrow without reducing the staker’s internal accounted balance. This enables a staker to later be deemed “inactive” (via requireInactiveStaker) and withdraw funds that were already sent to escrow, leading to contract insolvency and making honest validators unable to withdraw. The user’s H-01 scenario leverages exactly this path and adds that a reduced requiredStake amplifies the insolvency. Fixing the accounting bug in #24 (decreasing the staker’s balance when transferring to loserStakeEscrow or otherwise preventing such double-withdraw) would also fix H-01’s exploit path. Both issues target the same functions (stakeOnNewAssertion non-first-child branch and the inactivity/withdrawal flow) and an attack that works for one works through the same mechanism, so the vulnerability was detected.
